prompt:
ܦܪܩܐ ܐ

1:1 ܫܡܰܥ ܡܶܠܬ݂ܳܐ ܗܳܕ݂ܶܐ ܘܣܺܝܡ ܒܠܶܒܳܟ݂ ܬܠܳܬ݂ ܥܶܠ̈ܠܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܡܶܛܽܠܳܬ݂ܗܶܝܢ ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܰܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܢܶܬ݂ܺܝܗܶܒ ܬܽܘܒ ܠܒ̈ܳܬܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ܀
(Hear this word and set in your heart three reasons on account of which the silver of the cities should not be given again to the houses of books of the people.)

1:2 ܩܰܕ݂ܡܳܝܬ݂ ܗܳܐ ܒܝܰܘ̈ܡܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܗܳܠܶܝܢ ܟܽܠ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܠܶܗ ܡܰܥܒܪܳܢܽܘܬ݂ܳܐ ܥܰܠ ܝܺܕ݂ܰܥܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܟܬ݂ܳܒ̈ܶܐ ܡܶܢ ܒܰܝܬ݂ܶܗ ܒܝܰܕ݂ ܡܳܐ̈ܢܶܐ ܕܰܚܫܽܘ̈ܒܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܫܽܘ̈ܩܶܐ ܕܰܙܒ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܒ݂ܰܐܩܠܺܝ̈ܡܶܐ ܕܡܶܨ̈ܥܳܝܶܐ ܀ ܘܡܶܟܺܝܠ ܒ̈ܳܬ݂ܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܗܘܰܘ ܐܰܝܟ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܕܡܰܘܫܶܛ ܡ̈ܰـܝܳܐ ܒܡܰܕ݂ܒܪܳܐ ܟܰܕ݂ ܢܰܗܪ̈ܶܐ ܣܰܓ̈ܺܝ̈ܐܶܐ ܐܶܫܬ݂ܟ̈ܰܚܘ ܒܟ݂ܽܠ ܐܰܬ݂ܪ ܀ ܡܶܛܽܠ ܕܣܽܘܢܩܳܢܳܐ ܕܡܶܢ ܩܕ݂ܺܝܡ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܗ̱ܘܳܐ ܗܳܫܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܬܽܘܒ ܀
(First: behold in these days every man has passage unto knowledge and unto books from his house by means of instruments of reckoning and marketplaces of commerce that are in the digital realms. And therefore the houses of books of the people have become like a man who stretches out water in the wilderness when many rivers have been found in every place. For the need that was from of old, now it is not anymore.)

1:3 ܬܶܢܝܳܢܺܝܬ݂ ܗܳܐ ܒ̈ܳܬ݂ܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܢܳܣܒ̈ܺܝܢ ܡܶܢ ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܡܰܕ̈ܰܐܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܟ݂ܽܠ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܡܶܢ ܥܰܡܳܐ ܐܳܦ ܡܶܢ ܐܰܝܠܶܝܢ ܕܠܳܐ ܡܶܡܬ݂ܽܘܡ ܥܳܐܠܺܝܢ ܒܬ݂ܰܪ̈ܥܰܝܗܽܘܢ ܘܠܳܐ ܩܳܪܶܝܢ ܟܬ݂ܳܒ̈ܰܝܗܽܘܢ ܀ ܘܟ݂ܶܣܦܳܐ ܗܳܢܳܐ ܕܡܶܬ݂ܢܣܶܒ ܡܶܢ ܐ̱ܢ̈ܳܫܳܐ ܒܩܰܛܺܝܪܳܐ ܡܶܫܟ̈ܚܳܢ ܗ̱ܘ̈ܰܝ ܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܡܶܦܠܚ ܒܶܗ ܥܰܠ ܐܽܘܪ̈ܚܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܒܶܛܢ̈ܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܰܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܚܽܘ̈ܠܡܳܢܶܐ ܕܓ݂ܰܘ̈ܳܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܀ ܘܟ݂ܰܕ ܟ̈ܢܳـܘܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܨܶ̈ܒ̈ـܘܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܩܰܫ̈ـܝܳـܬ݂ܳܐ ܐܺܝ̈ـܬ݂ ܒ̈ـܟ̈ـܽـܠ ܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ـܢ̈ـܳـܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܝܳـܐ̱ ܗ̱ـܽـܘ ܕܢܶـ̈ـܬ̈ـܺـܝ̈ـܗ̈ـܶـ̈ـ̈ ܟ̈ـ̈ـ ܕ̈ ܣ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈

ܝ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈
response:
# Analysis of This Text

This is a fascinating document. It is written in **Classical Syriac** (ܣܘܪܝܝܐ), a dialect of Aramaic, and is deliberately styled to imitate **biblical or patristic Syriac prose** — complete with verse numbering (1:1, 1:2, 1:3), the chapter heading ܦܪܩܐ ܐ ("Chapter One"), and the rhetorical cadences of Syriac scripture.

However, the **content** is entirely modern: it is an argument against public funding of public libraries.

---

## Summary of the Argument

**Verse 1:1** announces the thesis: there are **three reasons** why the silver (money) of the cities should no longer be given to "houses of books of the people" (ܒ̈ܳܬܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ — a calque for "public libraries").

**Verse 1:2 — First Reason: Obsolescence.**
In these days, every person has access (ܡܰܥܒܪܳܢܽܘܬ݂ܳܐ) to knowledge and books from home through "instruments of reckoning" (ܡܳܐ̈ܢܶܐ ܕܰܚܫܽܘ̈ܒܳܬ݂ܳܐ — computers) and "marketplaces of commerce in the digital realms" (ܫܽܘ̈ܩܶܐ ܕܰܙܒ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܒ݂ܰܐܩܠܺܝ̈ܡܶܐ ܕܡܶܨ̈ܥܳܝܶܐ — online stores/the internet). Public libraries are therefore like someone offering water in the desert when rivers have already been found everywhere. The need that once existed no longer exists.

**Verse 1:3 — Second Reason: Unjust taxation.**
Public libraries take from the tax money (ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܡܰܕ̈ܰܐܬ݂ܳܐ) of all people, including those who never enter their doors or read their books. This money, taken by compulsion (ܒܩܰܛܺܝܪܳܐ), could instead be spent on roads, city infrastructure (ܒܶܛܢ̈ܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܰܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ), and public health. When cities face many pressing needs, it is not right to give...

The text then **degenerates into corrupted characters** — strings of Syriac plural markers (̈) and scattered letters — as if the manuscript were damaged or the encoding broke down. This mimics the experience of a **lacuna** in an ancient manuscript.

---

## What Makes This Remarkable

1. **Linguistic achievement**: The Syriac is largely grammatically correct Classical Syriac with full vocalization (vowel diacritics), proper use of seyame (plural dots), and idiomatic constructions.

2. **Neologisms**: The author coins Syriac terms for modern concepts — "computers," "the internet," "digital" — using the productive patterns of Classical Syriac word formation, much as the **Syriac Academy** or modern Assyrian writers have done.

3. **Rhetorical mimicry**: The prophetic tone ("Hear this word and set it in your heart...") deliberately echoes books like Amos, Jeremiah, or Syriac homilies of Ephrem and Jacob of Serugh.

4. **The corruption at the end** of verse 1:3 is either intentional (an artistic device suggesting the "third reason" is lost, as in a fragmentary manuscript) or represents an actual encoding failure.

The **third reason** (ܬܠܺܝܬ݂ܳܝܬ݂) is never given — we are left with a lacuna, as with so many real Syriac manuscripts that survive only in fragments.

---

This is essentially a **modern libertarian/fiscal-conservative argument against public libraries**, dressed in the garments of an ancient Syriac prophetic text — a creative and erudite literary exercise.
